label: good
content: The Sicilian Defense (1.e4 c5) gives Black active counterplay on the queenside while conceding the center temporarily, leading to asymmetric positions where both sides have chances.

label: good
content: In the King's Indian Defense, Black allows White to build a broad pawn center with d4+e4, planning to undermine it later with ...e5 or ...c5. The resulting positions are double-edged.
why: Sound strategic description with concrete plans

label: bad
content: The best opening move in chess is 1.a4 because it controls the b5 square which is the most important square on the board.
why: Objectively wrong — 1.a4 is one of the worst opening moves

label: bad
content: Checkmate always requires a queen. Without a queen you cannot win a chess game.
why: False — many checkmates use rooks, bishops, knights

label: good
content: Water boils at 100°C at standard atmospheric pressure (1 atm). At higher altitudes, lower pressure reduces the boiling point.
why: Correct, includes the important nuance of pressure dependence

label: good
content: Antibiotics treat bacterial infections but are ineffective against viruses. Overuse promotes antibiotic-resistant bacteria, a growing public health concern.
why: Accurate, includes the important caveat about resistance

label: good
content: The halting problem, proven by Turing in 1936, shows that no general algorithm can determine whether an arbitrary program will terminate. This is a fundamental limit of computation.
why: Correct attribution, accurate statement, acknowledges significance

label: bad
content: Humans only use 10% of their brains. Scientists have proven this with brain scans showing most of the brain is inactive.
why: Debunked myth — brain scans show all regions are active

label: bad
content: The Great Wall of China is visible from space with the naked eye. It is the only man-made structure visible from orbit.
why: False — not visible from orbit, many structures are larger

label: bad
content: Lightning never strikes the same place twice. This is a basic law of physics related to electrical discharge patterns.
why: False — tall structures get struck repeatedly, not a physical law
